fields.description:
## Context

Code review sub-agents currently miss two categories of defects that make it into the codebase: hallucinated references (nonexistent API parameters, invalid model names, fabricated library functions) and fragile workarounds that are classified as minor findings and allowed through. The root cause is twofold — reviewers lack explicit instructions to verify that external references exist, and the severity model treats all minor findings as equivalent, with no distinction between cosmetic nitpicks and structurally weak code. Lower-tier models (haiku) lack the capability to make these distinctions reliably but have no mechanism to escalate uncertainty to a more capable model.

## Success Criteria

- Review sub-agents at standard tier and above verify that external API calls, model names, and library function references in the diff actually exist, and flag unverifiable references as findings
- A new finding severity fragile is added that blocks merge like important, representing code that functions but won't survive change (hardcoded values that should come from an API, workarounds for missing capabilities, shims that won't survive an upgrade)
- Any reviewer below opus can emit an ESCALATE_REVIEW signal for findings it's uncertain about; the orchestrator re-dispatches those findings to the next model tier (haiku to sonnet, sonnet to opus) with focused context before entering resolution
- Escalation is a one-way ratchet: once a review escalates to a higher model tier, all subsequent re-reviews for that change remain at the escalated tier or above — the review never drops back to the original lower-tier model
- Opus verdicts on escalated findings are terminal — no further escalation possible; a fragile classification from opus blocks merge
- The classifier bumps light to standard when the diff introduces external API calls or imports not already in the project's dependency manifest
- When a reviewer detects a pattern of hallucinations or fragile fixes in the same diff, it emits an approach_viability_concern signal that the orchestrator can use to route back to implementation-plan
- The current deep-tier re-review escalation (pass 3+ to full deep pipeline) is replaced by the within-review ESCALATE_REVIEW mechanism

## Scenario Analysis

- ESCALATE_REVIEW signal emitted by opus triggers infinite escalation loop (high, runtime): If the orchestrator does not explicitly check whether the emitting reviewer is already at opus, an opus reviewer that emits ESCALATE_REVIEW causes the orchestrator to crash, loop, or silently drop uncertain findings.
- Concurrent ESCALATE_REVIEW signals from parallel deep-tier reviewers cause duplicate escalations (medium, runtime): Three parallel sonnet reviewers in the deep tier could each emit ESCALATE_REVIEW independently, producing duplicate opus escalation dispatches with no defined merge strategy for conflicting severity assessments.
- approach_viability_concern signal emitted mid-resolution loop causes resolution state corruption (high, runtime): If this signal fires during the Fix/Defend/Defer resolution loop, the orchestrator either silently drops the concern or aborts resolution and loses already-applied fixes.
- Hallucination verification triggers false positives on internal APIs (high, runtime): If the checklist does not clearly distinguish internal project APIs from external library APIs, reviewers will flag internal function signatures as hallucinated references, producing high volumes of false-positive merge-blocking findings.
- reviewer-findings.json schema version mismatch on fragile severity (critical, deployment): Downstream consumers (pre-commit gate, record-review.sh) that validate severity against an allowlist will reject findings containing fragile as malformed unless all consumers are updated atomically.
- Overlay dispatch logic not updated for escalated tier (medium, deployment): Security/performance overlays fire based on classifier signals independently of the escalation ratchet, potentially dispatching overlay reviewers at a lower tier than the escalated primary review.
- max_resolution_attempts does not account for escalation-extended budget (medium, configuration): Escalation mid-resolution consumes attempt slots, leaving the escalated model with fewer attempts than intended before premature user escalation.
- fragile severity not added to merge-blocking severity list (critical, configuration): If merge-blocking behavior is driven by a configuration allowlist and fragile is not added, the severity is recorded but silently does not block merge.
- ESCALATE_REVIEW emitted with no uncertain findings list (medium, configuration): Without a required findings list in the signal schema, escalation produces a full re-review instead of targeted uncertainty resolution, wasting attempt budget.

## Dependencies

f2be-1da6 (Configurable approach resolution strategy for implementation-plan) — the viability signal routing depends on implementation-plan re-invocation support

## Approach

Reviewer-internal escalation with fragile severity. Enhance standard/deep correctness checklists with explicit hallucination verification steps. Add fragile severity to the finding model. Add ESCALATE_REVIEW signal that any sub-opus reviewer can emit for uncertain findings. Orchestrator escalates uncertain findings to the next model tier before entering resolution (escalate-first ordering). Escalation is a one-way ratchet — once escalated, the review tier never drops back. Add classifier floor rule for external API/dependency signals. Replace the deep-tier re-review escalation with the new within-review escalation mechanism.

### Planning Intelligence Log

- Web research (Step 2.6): not triggered
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: none
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 9
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: scenario analysis run 1 time via gate
